resolved.title: Spain. Lisbon water front & Plaza of Commerce

decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1998, Portugal, preparing for euro adoption. The government must decide whether to join the first wave of eurozone countries in 1999, giving up full control of its own monetary policy just as it had only recently stabilized its economy after decades of upheaval — including the 1974 Carnation Revolution and the loss of its African colonies. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: some European countries chose to delay or avoid euro adoption entirely, weighing the loss of currency independence against the benefits of full economic integration.Player choice: Join the euro in the first 1999 wave, or delay adoption to preserve monetary independence longer.Historical resolution: Portugal joined the euro in the first wave, with the escudo fixed at 200.482 per euro from December 31, 1998, and coins exchangeable until December 31, 2002.

understand.economic_impact: Portugal's decision to fix the escudo to the euro at 200.482 per euro and fully adopt the euro in the first 1999 wave required a significant fiscal consolidation effort, ending centuries of independent Portuguese monetary policy European Commission. The loss of African colonies in 1974–75 also meant the escudo's economic footprint shrank dramatically, from a currency used across multiple continents to one confined to mainland Portugal [PALOP decolonization timeline, master bible research].
understand.political_impact: The escudo's creation was a direct consequence of a revolution — Portugal's centuries-old monarchy ended in October 1910, and the new republic asserted its identity partly through a new currency the following year Wikipedia: 5 October 1910 revolution. Decolonization in the mid-1970s ended the escudo's use across Angola, Mozambique, and other former colonies, each adopting independent national currencies within a few years of independence Wikipedia: Angolan kwanza.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Portugal was once one of the most powerful countries in the world — its ships reached places no European had ever sailed to before, and its money and influence went with them across multiple continents. Centuries later, after losing a monarchy, an empire, and its African colonies, Portugal became one of Europe's smaller, quieter economies, and quietly chose to give up its own currency altogether for the euro. Ana's story matters because it shows that being smaller now doesn't erase having once been extraordinary — a country, like a person, can carry pride and loss for the same history at the same time, and that feeling has a name in Portuguese: saudade.
